title: Legislative Horizon Tracker
desc: The Legislative Horizon Tracker monitors enacted legislation, commencement orders, statutory instruments, and regulatory developments that affect or will affect the UK employment law landscape. All horizon intelligence carries the Tier 5 (Forward) disclaimer per AILANE-SPEC-PLUGIN-001 Article XIV §14.3. Confidence classifications follow the KLIA-001 Forward Intelligence Layer (FIL) framework: CONFIRMED (Royal Assent + commencement order made), HIGH (Royal Assent achieved, commencement pending), MEDIUM (Bill at advanced parliamentary stage), LOW (Bill introduced or consultation stage). This tracker is the primary data source for the Regulatory Ticker and Legislative Alerts delivered to Operational and Governance tier subscribers.
disclaimerTier: Tier 5 (Forward) — Pre-enactment and pre-commencement analysis. Confidence classification mandatory. Subject to change. Royal Assent caveat where applicable.

events.event: Commencement No. 1 — Trade Union and Industrial Action Provisions
events.commencement: The Employment Rights Act 2025 (Commencement No. 1 and Transitional and Saving Provisions) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/3)
provisions: s.61 — Removal of requirement for 10-year political fund ballots
s.62 — Removal of requirement to remind members of opt-out right; switch to opt-out model for new members
s.63 — Removal of prohibition on public sector check-off
s.64–69 — Simplified ballot and notice requirements for industrial action
s.70 — Reduction of industrial action notice period from 14 to 10 days
s.71 — Simplified information on ballot papers
s.72 — Extension of industrial action mandate from 6 to 12 months
s.73 — Removal of requirement for picket supervisor
s.76 — Protection from detriment to deter industrial action (commenced but regulations required for practical effect)
s.77 — Removal of 12-week protected period for industrial action dismissal — automatically unfair at any time
s.80–86 — Various trade union governance provisions
events.aceiImpact: Trade Union & Industrial Action category. Significant shift in the balance of industrial relations. Employers with recognised trade unions face extended mandate periods, simplified ballot processes, and unlimited protection against dismissal for industrial action. FM sector employers with unionised workforces directly affected.
events.employerAction: Review processes for handling industrial action. Update manager training on trade union rights. Review check-off arrangements. Ensure constructive relations with recognised unions.

events.event: April 2026 Commencement — Family Leave, SSP, and FWA
events.commencement: The Employment Rights Act 2025 (Commencement No. 2 and Transitional and Saving Provisions) (Amendment) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/323)
provisions: s.14 — Paternity leave becomes day-one right (26-week qualifying period removed)
s.15 — Parental leave becomes day-one right (1-year qualifying period removed)
s.16–17 — Paternity leave may now be taken after shared parental leave
s.19 — Statutory Sick Pay: removal of 3-day waiting period (day-one SSP)
s.20 — Statutory Sick Pay: removal of Lower Earnings Limit (LEL) threshold — all workers eligible
s.20 — SSP for low earners: 80% of normal weekly earnings or flat rate (£123.25/week), whichever is lower
s.41 — Whistleblowing protections strengthened for sexual harassment disclosures
s.55 — Bereaved Partners' Paternity Leave (up to 52 weeks if mother/primary adopter dies within first year)
Fair Work Agency (FWA) established 7 April 2026 — enforcement powers to follow via further regulations
Equality Action Plans — voluntary for employers with 250+ employees from April 2026
Collective redundancy protective award — maximum period doubled
events.aceiImpact: Family Leave, Pay & Compensation, Whistleblowing, Dismissal categories all affected. Day-one paternity and parental leave removes the historical buffer period. SSP changes affect payroll and absence management. FWA establishment signals intensified enforcement.
events.employerAction: Update paternity leave and parental leave policies — remove qualifying period references. Amend contracts referencing SSP eligibility. Recalculate SSP for low earners. Update payroll systems. Review whistleblowing policy for sexual harassment disclosures. Prepare for FWA inspections.

events.event: October 2026 Commencement — Zero Hours, Fire and Rehire, Guaranteed Hours
events.status: Expected — commencement order not yet made
provisions: Part 1 — Zero hours workers: right to guaranteed hours after qualifying reference period
Part 1 — Right to reasonable notice of shifts and compensation for short-notice cancellation
Fire and rehire restrictions — employer must follow enhanced consultation requirements before dismissing and re-engaging on new terms
Duty to inform workers of their right to join a trade union
Strengthened trade union right of access to workplaces
Requirement to take 'all' reasonable steps (not just 'reasonable steps') to prevent sexual harassment — strengthening the Worker Protection Act 2023 duty
Third-party harassment — new obligation on employers not to permit harassment of employees by third parties
events.aceiImpact: Working Arrangements, Dismissal Procedures, Discrimination, Trade Union categories. Zero hours reforms fundamentally change the relationship with flexible workforce. Fire and rehire restrictions increase the cost and complexity of terms-change programmes. Enhanced sexual harassment duty raises the compliance bar.
events.employerAction: Audit zero hours workforce. Prepare guaranteed hours offer processes. Review fire and rehire policies. Update sexual harassment prevention measures to meet 'all reasonable steps' standard. Prepare for third-party harassment obligations.

events.event: January 2027 — Unfair Dismissal Qualifying Period Reduction
events.status: Government confirmed — commencement order not yet made
provisions: Reduction of unfair dismissal qualifying period from 2 years to 6 months
Applies to employees already in employment at 1 January 2027 — anyone hired from ~end of June 2026 onwards will gain protection
Removal of statutory power to change qualifying period by order — future changes require primary legislation
Removal of compensation cap on unfair dismissal — currently £118,223 or 52 weeks' pay (whichever lower)
Introduction of 'initial period of employment' with light-touch dismissal process (details in secondary legislation)
events.aceiImpact: Dismissal Procedures category — transformational change. Employer exposure increases dramatically. Combined with compensation cap removal, the financial risk of unfair dismissal claims increases by an order of magnitude for employers with high earners. Every employer needs to review probationary period arrangements and performance management processes.
events.employerAction: Review and implement robust probationary period processes. Ensure all dismissals from June 2026 onwards are procedurally sound. Prepare for increased tribunal claim volumes. Review insurance and legal budget provisions. Train managers on fair dismissal procedure for the new qualifying period.

events.event: National Minimum Wage and National Living Wage rate increases — April 2026
provisions: National Living Wage (21+): £12.71/hour (from £12.21)
16–17 and apprentice rate: £8.00/hour (from £7.55)
Accommodation offset: £11.10/day (from £10.66)
events.aceiImpact: Pay & Compensation category. Payroll systems must be updated. FM sector employers with high volumes of NMW/NLW workers particularly affected.
events.employerAction: Update payroll systems for new rates effective 1 April 2026. Audit all worker pay rates to ensure compliance. Review accommodation offset calculations. Update worker pay rate communications.

provisions: Statutory Sick Pay: £123.25/week (from £118.75)
Statutory maternity, adoption, paternity, shared parental, neonatal care and parental bereavement pay: £194.32/week (from £187.18)
Maximum compensatory award for unfair dismissal: £123,543 (from £118,223) — NB: ERA 2025 proposes to remove this cap entirely
A week's pay for basic award and statutory redundancy payment: £751 (from £719)
Maximum basic award: £22,530 (£751 × 30)
Statutory redundancy pay maximum: £22,530
events.aceiImpact: Pay & Compensation, Dismissal Procedures categories. All compensation calculations for tribunal claims must use the new figures for dismissals on or after 6 April 2026.
events.employerAction: Update payroll systems. Update template documents referencing statutory rates. Recalculate any pending redundancy payments. Update risk exposure assessments for potential tribunal claims.

events.instrument: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
events.event: Preventive duty to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment — In Force
provisions: New EqA 2010 s.40A — proactive employer duty to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment
Compensation uplift of up to 25% where duty is breached and sexual harassment claim succeeds
Not a standalone claim — applies only where an underlying sexual harassment claim under EqA 2010 s.26(2) succeeds
ERA 2025 will strengthen this from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' (expected October 2026)
events.aceiImpact: Discrimination category. Employer must demonstrate proactive prevention measures. Risk assessments, training, reporting channels, and swift action on complaints are minimum expectations.
events.employerAction: Conduct sexual harassment risk assessment. Implement or refresh anti-harassment training. Establish clear and accessible reporting channels. Document all preventive measures taken. Prepare for strengthening to 'all reasonable steps' under ERA 2025.

events.instrument: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023
events.event: Enhanced redundancy protection during pregnancy and family leave — In Force
provisions: Priority right to suitable alternative vacancy extended from maternity leave period to pregnancy period and 18 months post-birth
Applies to maternity leave, adoption leave, and shared parental leave
Employer must offer suitable alternative vacancy before the existing contract ends — positive obligation, not right to compete
ERA 2025 contains further strengthening provisions (not yet in force)
events.aceiImpact: Family Leave, Dismissal Procedures categories. Extended protection period significantly increases the window during which pregnant employees and new parents have priority in redundancy situations.
events.employerAction: Update redundancy procedures to check for pregnancy/family leave status before finalising selection. Train managers on extended protection periods. Update HR systems to flag protected employees in redundancy exercises.

events.event: Enhanced flexible working request rights — In Force
provisions: Day-one right to request flexible working (26-week qualifying period removed)
Two requests per 12-month period (was one)
Decision period reduced from 3 months to 2 months
Requirement for employee to explain effect on employer removed
Employer must consult with employee before refusing
ACAS Code of Practice 5 on Flexible Working issued alongside
events.aceiImpact: Working Arrangements category. Increased volume of flexible working requests expected. Shortened decision period requires faster internal processes.
events.employerAction: Update flexible working policy to reflect day-one right and two-request limit. Train managers on the ACAS Code of Practice 5. Ensure consultation process is documented before any refusal.

surveillanceStatus.note: The horizon tracker is the primary surveillance target. Every commencement order, SI, and regulatory development requires assessment against the existing event set. ERA 2025 commencement is the highest-priority monitoring stream.
pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 Commencement No. 3 — expected mid-2026 (zero hours, fire and rehire provisions)
ERA 2025 Commencement No. 4 — expected October 2026 (trade union access, harassment strengthening)
ERA 2025 Commencement No. 5 — expected January 2027 (unfair dismissal qualifying period, compensation cap)
NMW/NLW rates — annual April update cycle
Statutory rates — annual April update cycle

constitutionalBasis.ACEI: ACEI Constitution Art. V §5.1 — Forward Intelligence obligation. The ACEI must incorporate enacted but uncommenced legislation into exposure calculations using confidence-weighted projections.
constitutionalBasis.RRI: RRI Constitution Art. IV §4.2 — Regulatory readiness requires horizon scanning of pending legislative and regulatory changes.
constitutionalBasis.CCI: CCI Constitution Art. III §3.3 — Conduct assessment must account for employer preparedness for upcoming regulatory obligations.

confidenceFramework.CONFIRMED: Royal Assent achieved AND commencement order made. In force or coming into force on specified date.
confidenceFramework.HIGH: Royal Assent achieved. Commencement pending — government has indicated timeline.
confidenceFramework.MEDIUM: Bill at advanced parliamentary stage (Report Stage or later). Royal Assent anticipated.
confidenceFramework.LOW: Bill introduced or consultation stage. Outcome uncertain.

governingArchitecture.dataGovernance: All horizon events are sourced from legislation.gov.uk, Hansard, and official government publications. No speculation. Confidence classification mandatory for every event. Employer action guidance is procedural, not advisory — consistent with Clinical Neutrality Standard (ACEI Constitution Art. I §1.4).
governingArchitecture.disclaimerTier: Tier 5 (Forward) — Pre-enactment and pre-commencement analysis. Confidence classification mandatory. Subject to change. Royal Assent caveat where applicable.
